title: Algorithmic Thresholds for Mean-Field Spin Glasses
speaker: Mark Sellke
abstract: I will explain recent progress on computing approximate ground states of mean-field spin glass Hamiltonians, which are certain random functions in high dimension. While the asymptotic ground state energy OPT is given by the famous Parisi formula, these functions often have many bad local optima which are believed to impede simple algorithms such as gradient descent. In the positive direction, I will discuss algorithms based on approximate message passing which asymptotically achieve a value ALG given by an extended Parisi formula.

proof_stages.label: Mixed p-spin Hamiltonians and random-optimization framing
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines mixed mean-field spin glass Hamiltonians as high-dimensional Gaussian polynomial processes specified by a mixture covariance, restricts them to cube or sphere state spaces, and frames their ground states as average-case random optimization problems.

proof_stages.label: Parisi optimum and the algorithmic problem
proof_stages.summary: The zero-temperature Parisi variational formula gives the limiting optimum OPT, but its nonconstructive nature and the rugged low-temperature landscape motivate asking for efficient algorithms reaching OPT or a precise threshold below it.

proof_stages.label: Ultrametric tree and no-overlap-gap heuristic
proof_stages.summary: Ultrametricity of Gibbs samples suggests a hierarchical tree of near-optimal states, where full branching/no overlap gap permits descent by small local steps while gaps force macroscopic jumps that should cause algorithmic loss.

proof_stages.label: Spherical Hessian-walk algorithm and ALG value
proof_stages.summary: For spherical spin glasses, the Hessian top-eigenvector walk takes small approximately orthogonal steps whose accumulated GOE eigenvalue gains give an explicit ALG threshold, matching OPT exactly in the concave/no-overlap-gap regimes.

proof_stages.label: Nonmonotone Parisi relaxation and AMP attainment on the cube
proof_stages.summary: For Ising/cube models, ALG is defined by minimizing the Parisi functional over a relaxed nonmonotone class, and an approximate-message-passing/martingale algorithm driven by the associated PDE and stochastic control problem attains that value.

proof_stages.label: Stable-algorithm obstruction from the overlap gap property
proof_stages.summary: The classical OGP interpolation argument shows that stable algorithms cannot reach asymptotic optimality unless ALG already equals OPT, because continuity of outputs along correlated Hamiltonian paths forces a forbidden overlap.

proof_stages.label: Branching OGP and Lipschitz hardness at ALG
proof_stages.summary: The strengthened branching OGP plus overlap concentration for Lipschitz algorithms builds arbitrary ultrametric solution trees from correlated Hamiltonian trees, and a Parisi-type upper-bound argument shows such trees are impossible above ALG, making ALG the sharp Lipschitz algorithmic threshold.
